title: Hegseth Refuses to Reaffirm US Article 5 Commitment — 'That's the President's Decision'
detail: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth declined to reaffirm US commitment to NATO's Article 5 collective defense obligation at a Pentagon briefing. When asked by Reuters whether the US remained committed to Article 5, Hegseth responded: 'As far as NATO is concerned, that's a decision that will be left to the president.' The statement was described as 'extraordinary' by European officials — no US Defense Secretary had previously conditioned Article 5 commitment on presidential discretion rather than treating it as a fixed treaty obligation. GV Wire and Eutoday both headlined the refusal as a pivotal moment exposing NATO fault lines. European diplomats privately noted the statement appeared to make Article 5 reliability a day-to-day presidential prerogative rather than a constitutional treaty commitment. Trump simultaneously told reporters he was 'strongly considering' pulling the United States out of NATO, calling the alliance a 'paper tiger' over allies' Iran war refusal.

media.caption: Hegseth refuses to reaffirm US Article 5 commitment

title: France, Germany, Poland Issue Weimar Triangle Statement Reaffirming European Security
detail: The foreign ministers of France, Germany, and Poland issued a joint Weimar Triangle statement reaffirming their collective commitment to European security and the transatlantic alliance. The statement emphasized the NATO Eastern Sentry initiative, air and missile defense strengthening along NATO's eastern flank, and continued support for Ukraine's air defense against Russian aggression. Germany's government spokesperson separately described Trump's behavior as 'a recurring phenomenon' while reaffirming Berlin's commitment to NATO. The statement was issued via the German Federal Foreign Office and represented the most significant multilateral European coordination response to the Article 5 uncertainty created by Trump's Miami forum remarks and Hegseth's Pentagon briefing.

media.caption: France, Germany, Poland issue Weimar Triangle statement reaffirming European security
